A top-to-bottom mobile redesign that made a utility app feel intentional.
MyFrontier app redesign — mobile screens showing dashboard, bill pay, and support flow

import HeroImage from '../../components/HeroImage.astro' import Meta from '../../components/Meta.astro' import LottiePlayer from '../../components/LottiePlayer.astro' import ProseGroup from '../../components/ProseGroup.astro' import heroSrc from '../../assets/work/myfrontier-app/frontier-app-kv.png' # MyFrontier App Redesign The MyFrontier app covered bill pay, account management, technician scheduling, and support — but the experience didn't hold together. Screens felt inconsistent, navigation was unclear, and users were hitting friction at exactly the moments they could least afford it. The redesign rebuilt it from the layout up. ## The Problem Utility apps live at the worst end of the emotional spectrum. Nobody opens a telecom app to browse. They open it because they have a question about their bill, because a technician is coming and they need to confirm the window, or because their internet is down and they need answers fast. In that context, the bar for design isn't delight — it's don't slow people down. The MyFrontier app had accumulated inconsistencies across years: menus that didn't follow a clear hierarchy, screen layouts that varied from flow to flow, and interactions that gave no feedback. There was no shared visual language, and no motion system at all. Every fix had to respect those real-world stakes — people in a bad moment shouldn't have to think about the interface. ## The Work As ADD, I owned layout design across multiple screens and defined visual hierarchy for core UI components: the navigation menu, the appointment calendar, and the selector patterns used throughout bill pay and account management. The goal was a consistent structural logic that could hold across very different types of tasks — from scheduling a technician visit to reviewing a payment history. Hierarchy decisions weren't cosmetic. Putting the right information at the top of each screen, sizing interactive elements to match confidence not just thumb reach, and grouping related controls consistently — these were the moves that made the app feel like a single product rather than a collection of individual screens. I built a motion design system to run alongside it. ### Interface Design ![Frontier app introduction screens](../../assets/work/myfrontier-app/frontier-intro.png) ![Dashboard view](../../assets/work/myfrontier-app/frontier-dashboard.png) ![Bill pay flow](../../assets/work/myfrontier-app/frontier-bill-pay.png) ### Motion Design Motion gave users immediate feedback that their actions went through. A coin animation confirmed a payment, a leaf marked a completed setup, and the intro sequence covered the initial load so the app felt responsive from the first tap.
![Support flow — tracking technician status through installation](../../assets/work/myfrontier-app/frontier-bill-support.png) ## What I Learned Each animation in the system was solving a specific problem, not adding polish. The coin confirmed that a payment actually processed — removing the uncertainty that makes people tap "submit" twice. The leaf gave setup completion a distinct visual endpoint, so users knew they were done rather than wondering if there was another step. The intro sequence wasn't a splash screen; it was time-buying cover for the initial data load, so the first interaction the user had with the app felt instant. That distinction matters more in a utility app than anywhere else. A brand experience can afford an animation that says "look at us." A telecom app can't — the user's trust is already conditional. The motion had to earn its place by doing something specific, and that constraint produced a tighter, more purposeful system than most projects get.
A configurator built to close the gap between the showroom floor and a screen.
Samsung Bespoke Design Studio — product configurator with carousel and colour selection interface

<Meta client="Samsung" role="Associate Design Director" agency="Razorfish" year={2024} />

<ProseGroup>

# Samsung Bespoke Design Studio

Samsung's Bespoke line lets customers configure appliances down to the panel finish. But the online experience wasn't delivering that same sense of control. We built a tool to change that, one that makes the customization process feel as considered as the hardware itself.

</ProseGroup>

<ImagePair>

![Desktop view of the Bespoke Design Studio](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/desktop-06.png)

![Mobile view of the Bespoke Design Studio](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/mobile-06.png)

</ImagePair>

<ProseGroup>

## The Problem

In-store, customers can see the exact finish, feel the panel, and make a call. Online, you're working with a screen. Samsung needed a digital customization experience that matched the confidence customers felt in person, without the friction that usually comes with complex configurators. I worked across UX, research, and engineering to define the strategy and interaction model.

</ProseGroup>

<ProseGroup>

## The Work

Every decision was grounded in research. User behavior analysis, competitor benchmarking, and usability studies shaped how we framed the problem and structured the solution. I led wireframing and prototyping through multiple iterations, working closely with engineering to refine the interaction model until the configurator felt easy to use.

</ProseGroup>

### Wireframes

<Carousel label="Bespoke configurator wireframes">

![Wireframe slide 1](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/slide1.webp)

![Wireframe slide 2](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/slide2.webp)

![Wireframe slide 3](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/model.webp)

![Wireframe slide 4 depth](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/depth.webp)

![Wireframe slide 4](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/slide4.webp)

![Wireframe slide 5](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/slide5.webp)

![Wireframe slide 6](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/slide6.webp)

</Carousel>

![User flow for Bespoke Kitchen](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/user-flow-bespoke-kitchen-2.jpg)

<ImagePair>

![Desktop configuration view](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/desktop-03b.png)

![Mobile configuration view](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/mobile-04b.png)

</ImagePair>

<ImagePair>

![Desktop detail view](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/desktop-04b.png)

![Mobile detail view](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/mobile-03b.png)

</ImagePair>

<ProseGroup>

### Design Execution

I led UI and interaction design across mobile and desktop. Motion was used to reinforce actions, educational touchpoints helped reduce hesitation, and the design system was built flexible enough to grow with the platform.

</ProseGroup>

![Kitchen configurator overview](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/ha-bespoke-kitchen-desktop-006.jpg)

![Product selection grid — choosing which appliances to configure](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/frame-37444.webp)

![Mobile product type selection — choosing between fridge models](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/frame-37443.webp)

<ImagePair>

![Configurator color selection — choosing panel finishes by zone](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/image-2.png)

![Configurator with full color panel — all four door zones visible](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/new-design.png)

</ImagePair>

<ImagePair>

![Desktop color picker close-up — panel finish options](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/frame-37442.webp)

![Mobile color picker — step 4 of the configuration flow](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/frame-37445.webp)

</ImagePair>

![Final checkout step — selected configuration with pricing](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/desktop-13.png)

<ProseGroup>

## What I Learned

The hardest part of this project was sequencing. A customer configuring a fridge has to pick a model, then a size, then colors for four separate door panels. Show all of that at once and people bail. Show too little and they don't understand their options. Most of the design work was figuring out the right order to present decisions so each step felt obvious. That's what made the configurator work.

</ProseGroup>
Design system and GNB for one of the world's most-visited consumer electronics sites.
Samsung.com redesign — global navigation and product pages across desktop and mobile

import HeroImage from '../../components/HeroImage.astro'
import Meta from '../../components/Meta.astro'
import ImagePair from '../../components/ImagePair.astro'
import ProseGroup from '../../components/ProseGroup.astro'
import heroSrc from '../../assets/work/samsung-redesign/revamp-kv.jpg'

<HeroImage src={heroSrc} alt="Samsung.com Redesign — project showcase" />

<Meta client="Samsung" role="Senior Designer" agency="Razorfish" year={2023} />

<ProseGroup>

# Samsung.com Redesign

Samsung.com is one of the most-visited consumer electronics sites on the internet. The design system had accumulated debt across years of regional additions, and the global navigation was cracking under the weight of it. This engagement tackled both: tighten the system, then redesign the GNB so it could actually hold across every market Samsung operates in.

</ProseGroup>

<ProseGroup>

## The Problem

When a navigation system has to work across 12+ markets, every structural decision has downstream consequences. Different product catalogues mean different category hierarchies. Different languages mean different string lengths and text overflow behaviours. Right-to-left scripts flip the entire spatial logic of the layout. Market-specific regulatory requirements add items that don't exist elsewhere. The GNB couldn't just look like Samsung -- it had to accommodate all of that without becoming a regional patchwork. The existing system had grown that way organically, and it showed. The challenge was designing a navigation architecture strict enough to read as a single coherent brand at global scale, but flexible enough that no market needed to break it to make it work.

</ProseGroup>

<ProseGroup>

## The Work

As a Senior Designer on a large cross-functional team at Razorfish, I worked across three areas: evolving and applying the design system to key site sections, designing page layouts to Samsung's precise brand standards, and proposing and iterating on GNB updates.

The GNB work was the hardest part. The competing constraints were real: Samsung's brand guidelines are rigid by design, but market needs vary significantly, and engineering teams building across regions need structural predictability to ship without regressions. Every round of GNB iteration had to be stress-tested against those pressures -- not just against a single locale in Korean or English, but against the full range of script directions, catalogue depths, and nav item counts that different markets required. That meant a lot of rounds. Each one clarified the constraints more than the last.

</ProseGroup>

![UI mosaic overview](../../assets/work/samsung-redesign/revamp-ui-mosaic.jpg)

### Design System

<ImagePair>

![Product category page](../../assets/work/samsung-redesign/samsung-section-2-pcd-desktop.jpg)

![Product detail zoom](../../assets/work/samsung-redesign/samsung-section-2-pd-zoom-desktop.jpg)

</ImagePair>

![Product showcase](../../assets/work/samsung-redesign/samsung-section-4-showcase-desktop.jpg)

![Product detail page](../../assets/work/samsung-redesign/samsung-section-5-pd-desktop.jpg)

![Product finder](../../assets/work/samsung-redesign/samsung-section-6-pf-desktop.jpg)

### Global Navigation

![Global navigation bar — mobile category browsing](../../assets/work/samsung-redesign/samsung-section-3-gnb-desktop.jpg)

<ImagePair>

![Global navigation closeup](../../assets/work/samsung-redesign/samsung-section-3-gnb-closeup-desktop.jpg)

![Global navigation full desktop](../../assets/work/samsung-redesign/samsung-section-3-gnb-desktop-full.jpg)

</ImagePair>

<ProseGroup>

## What I Learned

The GNB problem was an information architecture problem before it was a visual one. The visual constraints -- Samsung's brand rigidity, the sparse premium aesthetic -- were actually the easier half. The harder question was structural: how do you build a nav hierarchy that holds when the product catalogue in one market has three tiers and another has five?

The answer was designing for the hardest case first. Rather than starting with the standard English-language layout and adapting outward, the iteration process had to treat the most complex market requirements as the baseline. That forced decisions about hierarchy depth, label truncation, and overflow behaviour that a single-locale design would never surface.

What this project reinforced: at global scale, the designer's job is to write constraints that regional teams can work within, not to design every regional variant themselves. A system that requires exceptions is a system that will drift. The goal was a GNB with so few ambiguous cases that the exceptions never needed to happen.

</ProseGroup>
Design system foundations for one of the largest pharmacy retailers in the country.
CVS Shop website redesign — e-commerce homepage, navigation, and design system components

import HeroImage from '../../components/HeroImage.astro'
import Meta from '../../components/Meta.astro'
import ImagePair from '../../components/ImagePair.astro'
import ProseGroup from '../../components/ProseGroup.astro'
import heroSrc from '../../assets/work/cvs-redesign/cvs-header-2.jpg'

<HeroImage src={heroSrc} alt="CVS Shop Website Redesign — project showcase" />

<Meta client="CVS" role="Associate Design Director" agency="Razorfish" year={2023} />

<ProseGroup>

# CVS Shop Website Redesign

When the system is right, everything downstream gets better. CVS.com is one of the largest pharmacy retailer e-commerce platforms in the US, serving customers who might be filling a prescription, buying skincare, or both in the same session. The shop had accumulated years of inconsistency — unclear hierarchy, fractured navigation, and a visual language that had drifted from the brand. The modernization was about restoring clarity, not reinventing the wheel.

</ProseGroup>

<ProseGroup>

## The Problem

CVS sits at an unusual intersection: it's both a pharmacy and a general retailer, which means the platform has to serve a wider range of intent than most e-commerce sites. Over time, the shop grew through acquisitions and incremental additions rather than deliberate system thinking. The result was a front-end that felt fractured — type scales that didn't hold up across pages, icons that varied in weight and proportion, and components that had been built independently rather than from shared parts.

Fixing it wasn't a greenfield problem. CVS.com is a live platform with entrenched brand equity and real customer volume. The constraints were real: the brand wasn't up for reinvention, and large-scale disruption to existing templates wasn't viable. What the work called for was a disciplined approach to foundations — define the system clearly, build it rigorously, and give the product teams something they could actually use.

</ProseGroup>

<ProseGroup>

## The Work

I worked from the ground up: typographic framework first, then icons, then components and templates. Starting with typography wasn't arbitrary. On a platform this size, type decisions affect nearly every surface. Getting the scale, weight, and hierarchy right before building components meant the components inherited consistency rather than having to engineer it in later.

The icon system came next because iconography is where visual inconsistency is most visible at a glance. A grid-based construction system ensured every icon held its proportions whether it appeared at 16px or 48px. Using CVS brand red as the primary icon color grounded the system in the existing brand rather than introducing something foreign to it.

The component library was built to be specific enough to be useful and flexible enough to scale. Homepage modules, product page layouts, and category navigation were all scoped to desktop and mobile from the start — not adapted after the fact.

</ProseGroup>

<ProseGroup>

### Typography & Icons

I designed a custom icon and illustration system for CVS using their brand red. Each icon was built on a grid to keep proportions consistent at any size.

</ProseGroup>

![Brand visual language — CVS design elements and UI patterns](../../assets/work/cvs-redesign/group-10624.jpg)

<ImagePair>

![Icon and illustration system — geometric shapes built for the CVS brand](../../assets/work/cvs-redesign/frame-10558.png)

![Icon construction — grid-based build process for consistent sizing](../../assets/work/cvs-redesign/frame-10629.png)

</ImagePair>

<ProseGroup>

### Components & Templates

The component library covered everything from the homepage to product pages to category navigation. Each piece was built to work across desktop and mobile.

</ProseGroup>

![CVS.com homepage — desktop and mobile responsive layout](../../assets/work/cvs-redesign/frame-10669.png)

![Shop navigation — category browsing on desktop and mobile](../../assets/work/cvs-redesign/frame-10672.png)

![Brand page template — Skin Care / Neutrogena on mobile and desktop](../../assets/work/cvs-redesign/group-10604.png)

<ImagePair>

![UI components — headlines, product cards, and product tabs](../../assets/work/cvs-redesign/frame-10630.png)

![UI components — pagination, branding elements, and category navigation](../../assets/work/cvs-redesign/frame-10608.png)

</ImagePair>

<ProseGroup>

## What I Learned

This was foundation work, which is not the glamorous part of a redesign. But foundation work is where the most leverage is. When the typographic scale is resolved, product teams stop making ad hoc decisions about which size to use. When the icon system is consistent, no one's spending cycles debating stroke weight. The component library gave teams a shared vocabulary to build from rather than starting from scratch on every new surface.

What the system unlocked was speed downstream. Consistent inputs produce consistent outputs, and that frees up design and engineering capacity for harder problems. If there had been more runway, I would have pushed further into the checkout and pharmacy flows, where the visual debt was heavier and the stakes for usability are higher. But the foundations were right, and that's what makes the rest possible.

</ProseGroup>
